elements.sent-2411-003: Conditional risk measures and their associated risk contribution measures are commonly employed in finance and actuarial science for evaluating systemic risk and quantifying the effects of risk contagion.
elements.sent-2411-004: This paper introduces various types of contribution measures based on the MCoVaR, MCoES, and MMME studied in \cite{ortega2021 and \citet{das2018 to assess both the absolute and relative effects of a single risk when other risks in a group are in distress.}}
elements.sent-2411-005: The properties of these contribution risk measures are examined, and sufficient conditions for comparing these measures between two sets of random vectors are established using univariate and multivariate stochastic orders and stochastic dependence notions.
elements.sent-2411-006: Numerical examples are presented for validating the conditions.
elements.sent-2411-007: Finally, a real dataset from the cryptocurrency market is also utilized to analyze the contagion effect in terms of our proposed contribution measures.
elements.sent-2411-008: Keywords: Systemic risk; Risk contagion; Risk contribution measure; Spillover effects; Cryptocurrency market; Stochastic orders JEL Classification: G20, G21, G22, G31, C3.
elements.sent-2411-009: Risk measures are pivotal tools for quantifying potential losses in financial market and unknown risks in actuarial science.
elements.sent-2411-010: They map random variables that represent risks or losses to real numbers and are characterized by a suite of properties such as monotonicity, subadditivity, and positive homogeneity.
elements.sent-2411-011: Among the widely recognized risk measures are Value-at-Risk (VaR) and Expected Shortfall (ES), which are extensively discussed in the literature \citep{duffie1997,delbaen2000coherent,acerbi2002 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: In the realms of actuarial science and finance, risks are usually happening as unisolated events.
elements.sent-2411-013: Financial institutions, when subjected to adverse conditions, can initiate a domino effect, propagating losses throughout the market and potentially triggering systemic risk.
elements.sent-2411-014: Systemic risk is a critical concept in these fields, as it can lead to market failure and financial crises, with profound implications for the broader economy \citep{divcpinigaitiene2018,zhang2023systemic .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Hence, there is a need for novel conditional risk (co-risk) measures to quantify and evaluate systemic risk in financial systems.
elements.sent-2411-016: The Conditional Value-at-Risk (CoVaR), introduced by \citet{tobias2016 , measures the VaR of a particular asset under a certain level of systemic stress.}
elements.sent-2411-017: Analysis of theoretical properties of these co-risk measures and their applications in finance and insurance can be found in \cite{kritzman2010,asimit2016,feinstein2017,asimit2018systemic,lin2018,duarte2021,liu2021asymptotics,waltz2022vulnerability,yang2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, these co-risk measures can only assess the contagion effect from one entity to another entity, but cannot characterize the absolute or relative spillover effects, which lays the foundation for relevant research on proposing various risk contribution measures.
elements.sent-2411-019: The former is usually defined as the difference between conditional and unconditional risk measures, while the later is defined as the ratio between the difference-based contribution measure and the benchmark unconditional risk measure.
elements.sent-2411-020: For example, \citet{tobias2016 introduced the well-known difference-based and ratio-based contribution measures in terms of CoVaR when the conditional systemic event is taken as VaR at some fixed level.}
elements.sent-2411-021: From the perspective of stochastic orders and dependence structures, \citet{sordo2018 provided sufficient conditions for ranking CoVaR, CoES, and their risk contribution measures $\Delta {\rm CoVaR}$ and $\Delta {\rm CoES}$.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Recently, \cite{zhang2024stochastic introduced several types of ratio-based distortion risk contribution measures ($\Delta^{\rm R {\rm CoD}$) and examined sufficient conditions for comparing these measures.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Most of the above mentioned works only consider a single risk as the systemic risk event, which runs counter to the reality in financial market that there might be multiple risks collapsing simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-024: This scenario hinders the usage of the aforementioned systemic risk measures, which calls for the definitions of multivariate systemic risk measures.
elements.sent-2411-025: In fact, multivariate risks are attracting increasing attention from many researchers such as \cite{sun2018,lee2013,ling2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Let ${\bm X = (X_1,\cdots,X_n)$ represent a portfolio of risks.}
elements.sent-2411-027: Here, we assume $X_2,\cdots,X_n$ represent systemic risk, capturing the volatility and uncertainty of the entire system or market, rather than just the impact of any individual asset or event.
elements.sent-2411-028: The definition of multivariate marginal mean excess (in short, MMME) is initially introduced by \cite{das2018 and the asymptotic behavior is studied under suitable conditions within the framework of multivariate regular variation, hidden regular variation and asymptotic tail independence.}
elements.sent-2411-029: The multivariate CoVaR (in short, MCoVaR) and multivariate CoES (in short, MCoES) are firstly formally defined in \cite{ortega2021 to quantify the risk contagion of $X_2,\cdots,X_n$ spilled over to $X_1$.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Besides, the difference-based contribution risk measures are also introduced.
elements.sent-2411-031: Utilizing MCoVaR, MCoES and MMME, and their associated difference-based contribution measures (with unconditional risk measure as the benchmark), \cite{ortega2021 investigated sufficient conditions for implementing comparisons between two different sets of multivariate risk vectors.}
elements.sent-2411-032: In this paper, we revisit the concepts of MCoVaR, MCoES and MMME by proposing two types of multivariate contribution risk measures.
elements.sent-2411-033: The first kind belongs to the difference-based contribution risk measures, and the benchmark measure is chosen by the multivariate co-risk measures with median-based systemic events, which is commonly adopted in financial market.
elements.sent-2411-034: The corresponding difference-based contribution risk measures are different from the ones mentioned in \cite{ortega2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: The second kind pertains to the ratio-based multivariate contribution risk measures, which has never been seen in the current literature.
elements.sent-2411-036: With these newly proposed contribution measures, we examine sufficient conditions based on univariate and multivariate stochastic orders and stochastic dependence notions for comparing two different sets of multivariate risk vectors.
elements.sent-2411-037: We further present illustrative examples to verify the conditions and validate the main results.
elements.sent-2411-038: In addition, we employ these measures to analyze the risk contagion in cryptocurrency market.
elements.sent-2411-039: Section \ref{sec:pre reviews some basic concepts, including univariate and multivariate stochastic orders, copula functions, and some well-known (conditional) risk measures.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Section \ref{sec:mulcontri introduces several new definitions of multivariate systemic risk contribution measures based on MCoVaR, MCoES and MMME.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Section \ref{sec:mul_risk compares two different risk portfolios under these new measures, and establishes sufficient conditions for their comparisons.}
elements.sent-2411-042: Section \ref{sec:Empirical analyzes the risk contagion effect in the cryptocurrency market by computing and comparing these proposed contribution risk measures.}

elements.sent-2411-059: The Archimedean copula, a prevalent category within the family of copulas, is characterized by a generating function known as the Archimedean generator.
elements.sent-2411-060: The expression for an $n$-dimensional Archimedean copula is given by: \begin{equation}C_\psi (u_1, u_2, \ldots, u_n) = \psi^{-1} \left( \psi(u_1) + \psi(u_2) + \cdots + \psi(u_n) \right),\end{equation} where $\psi$ is a strictly decreasing function called the generating function, with its inverse denoted as $\psi^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-061: Prominent examples of Archimedean copulas include the Clayton, Gumbel, and Frank copulas, each employing distinct generating functions to model the dependencies among random variables.

elements.sent-2411-063: For an individual risk $X$ with distribution function $F_X$, the Expected Shortfall (ES) of $X$ at a given probability level $p\in(0,1)$ is defined as \begin{equation}{\rm ES}_{p}[X] = \frac{1}{1-p} \int_{p}^{1} {{\rm VaR}_t}[X] dt ,~~ p \in (0,1).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-064: Essentially, VaR represents the one-sided critical value of asset value loss over a certain holding period at a given confidence level, practically manifesting as an amount serving as the threshold.
elements.sent-2411-065: Compared to VaR, ES considers the magnitude of losses beyond the VaR threshold, making it a more comprehensive measure of risk.
elements.sent-2411-066: ES is particularly suitable when tail risk is of concern or when a more comprehensive risk assessment is needed.
elements.sent-2411-067: Besides, according to the Basel IV accords, the internal/advanced model approach is revised by replacing the VaR measure with the ES measure, which further highlights the importance of ES in solvency regulation; see \cite{kou2013external,zaevski2023basel .}
elements.sent-2411-068: In finance, the interconnections among entities' (e.g. banks or financial institutions) risks can lead to varying levels of systemic risk.
elements.sent-2411-069: To investigate the risk contagion of other individuals on one concerned entity, \citet{ortega2021 introduced a multivariate co-risk measure called MCoVaR as follows: \begin{equation}\mathrm{MCoVaR}_{\bm p }[X_{1}|X_{2},\ldots,X_{n}]= {\rm VaR}_{p_1} \left[ X_1 \bigg| \bigcap\limits_{j = 2}^n \left\{{X_j} > {\rm VaR}_{p_j}[X_j]\right\} \right],\end{equation} where $\bm p =(p_1,\dots,p_n)\in (0,1)^n$.}
elements.sent-2411-070: Clearly, MCoVaR is the VaR of the conditional distribution of $X_1$ at level $p_1$, given the joint systemic risk event $\{X_2 > {\rm VaR _{p_2 [X_2], ..., X_n > {\rm VaR _{p_n [X_n]\}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: As a direct generalization, the MCoES is further introduced in \citet{ortega2021 as follows: \begin{equation}\mathrm{MCoES}_{\bm p }[X_{1}|X_{2},\dots,X_{n}]= \frac{1}{1-p_1} \int_{p_1}^1 {\rm MCoVaR}_{t,p_2,\dots,p_n}[X_{1}|X_{2},\dots,X_{n}] dt.\end{equation}.}

elements.subsection-2411-006: Parameter estimation of the marginal distributions
elements.sent-2411-168: The Generalized Pareto Distribution (GPD) is widely applied in the insurance and finance sectors as a probability distribution for modeling extreme values \citep{embrechts2013modelling,castillo1997fitting .}
elements.sent-2411-169: Its distribution function is defined as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq_marginal F} F(x;\xi ,\beta ) = \left\{ \begin{array}{l} 1 - \left(1+\frac{\xi}{\beta}x \right)^{-\frac{1}{\xi}},~~\xi \ne 0 1 - {\rm exp}\left(-\frac{x}{\beta} \right),~~~~~\xi =0 \end{array} \right.,\end{equation} where $\xi$ is the shape parameter and $\beta$ is the scale parameter.}
elements.sent-2411-170: For each individual risk $X_i$ with distribution function $F_i$ and a confidential level $\alpha$, the GPD serves as a suitable approximation for the excess distribution function $\mathbb P(X_i - \alpha \leq x| X_i > \alpha)$.
elements.sent-2411-171: Consequently, the following approximation can be utilized: \begin{eqnarray}F_i(x) = F_i(\alpha) +\mathbb P(X_i-\alpha \leq x-\alpha| X_i>\alpha)\left( 1 - F_i(\alpha) \right) \approx F_i(\alpha) + F(x-\alpha;\xi_i,\beta_i)\left( 1 - F_i(\alpha) \right),~ x\geq \alpha.\end{eqnarray}.

elements.subsection-2411-007: Parameter estimation of the mixed copula
elements.sent-2411-184: As per Table \ref{tab:spearman and Table \ref{tab:kendall , it can be noted that the correlation coefficients between cryptocurrencies pairs are different, indicating that the interdependence among cryptocurrencies is significantly asymmetric.}}
elements.sent-2411-185: Hence, the dependence structure between these cryptocurrencies cannot be characterized by symmetric copulas.
elements.sent-2411-186: Besides, Figure \ref{fig:Scatterplot shows that any pair of these three cryptocurrencies have a strong positive dependence at extreme values (for both of the left and right tails).}
elements.sent-2411-187: Take these observations into consideration, we ought to use an asymmetric copula with non-zero left and right tail dependence coefficients to model the dependence structure between these cryptocurrencies.
elements.sent-2411-188: As a result, we employ a mixed copula model to characterize the dependence structure.
elements.sent-2411-189: The mixed copula model is widely used in the existing literature, like \cite{cai2014selection and \cite{zhu2023asymptotic .}}
elements.sent-2411-190: We utilize Gaussian copula in the mixed copula model to capture the asymmetry interdependence between the cryptocurrencies.
elements.sent-2411-191: However, it is well-known that Gaussian copula can not capture the tail dependence well because of its zeroed upper and lower tail dependence coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-192: Considering that the Gumbel copula and the Clayton copula have respectively positive upper and lower tail dependence coefficients, we use the Gumbel copula and the Clayton copula to capture the dependence in the left and right tails.

elements.sent-2411-207: Systemic risk measures, such as MCoVaR, MCoES and MMME, consistently surpass their unconditional counterparts, namely VaR and ES.
elements.sent-2411-208: This discrepancy underscores the heightened potential risk of individual assets when systemic risk is taken into account.
elements.sent-2411-209: These systemic risk measures capture the essence of how the performance of interconnected assets during periods of market extremity can amplify the risk profile of individual assets, thereby highlighting the domino effect and the propagation mechanisms of systemic risk.
elements.sent-2411-210: The implications are profound: there exists a pronounced risk spillover in the currency market, characterized by robust correlations among various currencies.
elements.sent-2411-211: Relying on unconditional risk measures in isolation may lead to an underestimation of the true risk exposure.
elements.sent-2411-212: Under majority risk measures, ETH emerges with the highest risk profile, while BTC exhibits a comparatively lower risk level, with XMR occupying an intermediate position.
elements.sent-2411-213: This indicates that within the cryptocurrency market, ETH is more susceptible to systemic risk impacts, marked by notably higher price volatility and risk exposure in comparison to the other two digital assets.
elements.sent-2411-214: Conversely, BTC displays relative stability, with a lower risk exposure and spill-over effects, underscoring its status as the cryptocurrency with the largest market share and its perceived stability.
elements.sent-2411-215: Across a spectrum of risk measures, the risk ranking among the three currencies exhibits a noticeable consistency.
elements.sent-2411-216: This uniformity in risk perception across different cryptocurrencies indicates a stable market assessment of risk.
elements.sent-2411-217: It implies that within the currency market, relative measures such as ratio-based risk contribution measures (\(\Delta^{\rm R}\) and \(\Delta^{\rm R-med}\)) can effectively capture the systemic risk's spillover effects.
elements.sent-2411-218: These measures offer a nuanced approach beyond the reliance on absolute risk metrics like MCoVaR, MCoES and MMME.
elements.sent-2411-219: For the difference-based risk contribution measures (\(\Delta\) and \(\Delta^{\mathrm{med }\)), the indicators derived from ES surpass those predicated on VaR.}
elements.sent-2411-220: Conversely, for the ratio-based risk contribution measures (\(\Delta^{\mathrm{R }\) and \(\Delta^{\mathrm{R-med }\)), the scenario inverts, with VaR-based measures taking precedence.}}
elements.sent-2411-221: This suggests that ES-based measures confer a higher significance to individual assets' role in the distribution of systemic risk, while VaR-based measures accentuate the individual assets' proportional contribution to systemic risk as a whole.
elements.sent-2411-222: The disparity underscores the imperative to strike a balance in the selection of risk metrics, contingent upon the goals of risk management and the prevailing market conditions.
elements.sent-2411-223: The ratio-based risk contribution measures (\(\Delta^{\rm R}\) and \(\Delta^{\rm R-med}\)) offer a advantage over other risk metrics by virtue of their capacity to articulate the systemic risk's spillover effect on individual assets via relative ratios.
elements.sent-2411-224: This methodology provides clarity on the comparative risk contributions of various assets within systemic risk frameworks.
elements.sent-2411-225: It affords a perspective that not only uncovers potential market extreme losses but also equips investors with a deeper comprehension and management of relative risk exposures across diverse market conditions.
elements.sent-2411-226: This is in contrast to the sole assessment of an asset's absolute risk level.
elements.sent-2411-227: Systemic risk plays a significant role in financial markets and portfolio management.
elements.sent-2411-228: This article delves into new tools for quantifying and analyzing systemic risk, with a specific emphasis on the absolute and relative spillover effects induced by systemic risk.
elements.sent-2411-229: Some comparison results are conducted based on these proposed measures for two different sets of multivariate vectors with the same or different copulas.
elements.sent-2411-230: The theoretical findings have been validated through numerical examples, demonstrating the applicability and effectiveness of the proposed measures.
elements.sent-2411-231: Furthermore, we implement these measures as well as some known ones to quantify the spillover effect in cryptocurrency market by considering three typical cryptocurrencies.

elements.sent-2411-285: The $n$-dimensional Gumbel copula is defined as follows: \begin{equation}C_\theta(u_1,\dots,u_n) = {\rm exp}\left\{ -\left[ \sum_{i=1}^n (-{\rm ln}~ u_i)^\theta \right]^\frac{1}{\theta} \right\},~ \theta>1,~{\bm u} \in [0,1]^n.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-286: Typically, it demonstrates positive right-tail dependency, implying that when one variable exhibits an extreme value in the right tail, there is a higher probability for the other variable to also have an extreme value in the right tail.
elements.sent-2411-287: The $n$-dimensional Clayton copula is defined as follows: \begin{equation}C_\theta(u_1,\dots,u_n) = \left[ \sum_{i=1}^n u_i^{-\theta} -n +1 \right]^{-\frac{1}{\theta}},~ \theta>0,~{\bm u} \in [0,1]^n.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-288: Therefore, the combination of the Gumbel copula and the Clayton copula can simulate asymmetric upper and lower tail dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-289: In addition to Archimedean copulas, there is another class of copula functions called elliptical copulas, such as the Gaussian copula.
elements.sent-2411-290: The Gaussian copula exhibits a certain degree of symmetry in terms of its dependence properties in the left and right tails.
elements.sent-2411-291: The multivariate Gaussian copula is defined as follows: \begin{equation}C_R(u_1,\dots,u_n) = \Phi_R\left( \Phi^{-1}(u_1),\dots,\Phi^{-1}(u_n) \right),\end{equation} where $\Phi^{-1}$ is the inverse of the standard univariate normal distribution function $\Phi$.. \end{Def.}
elements.sent-2411-292: The tail dependence coefficient is a measure of the dependence between random variables in the tails of their joint distribution.
elements.sent-2411-293: It is divided into the upper tail dependence coefficient and the lower tail dependence coefficient, which describe the dependence of random variables in the upper and lower tails of their joint distribution, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-294: Based on this concept, the notion of multivariate upper and lower tail dependence coefficients is introduced.
elements.sent-2411-295: The multivariate upper tail dependence coefficient is defined as: \begin{equation}\lambda_U^{S|\bar{S}} = \lim_{u \to 1^{-}} \mathbb{P}\left(\bigcap_{i \in S} \{F_{i}(X_i) > u\} \bigg | \bigcap_{j \in \bar{S}} \{F_{j}(X_j) > u\}\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-296: The multivariate lower tail dependence coefficient is defined as: \begin{equation}\lambda_L^{S|\bar{S}} = \lim_{u \to 0^{+}} \mathbb{P}\left(\bigcap_{i \in S} \{F_{i}(X_i) \leq u\} \bigg| \bigcap_{j \in \bar{S}} \{F_{j}(X_j) \leq u\}\right).\end{equation}. \end{Def.}

elements.sent-2411-301: Conditional risk measures and their associated risk contribution measures are commonly employed in finance and actuarial science for evaluating systemic risk and quantifying the effects of risk interactions.
elements.sent-2411-302: This paper introduces various types of contribution ratio measures based on the MCoVaR, MCoES, and MMME studied in \cite{ortega2021 and \citet{das2018 to assess the relative effects of a single risk when other risks in a group are in distress.}}
elements.sent-2411-303: The properties of these contribution risk measures are examined, and sufficient conditions for comparing these measures between two sets of random vectors are established using univariate and multivariate stochastic orders and statistically dependent notions.
elements.sent-2411-304: Numerical examples are presented to validate these conditions.
elements.sent-2411-305: Finally, a real dataset from the cryptocurrency market is used to analyze the spillover effects through our proposed contribution measures.
elements.sent-2411-306: Keywords: Systemic risk; \textcolor{blue {Conditional risk measures ; Spillover effects; Cryptocurrency market; Stochastic orders MSC 2010 Classification: Primary 90B25; Secondary 60E15, 60K10.}}
elements.sent-2411-307: JEL Classification: G20, G21, G22, G31, C3.
elements.sent-2411-308: In actuarial science and finance, risks are often not isolated events but are highly correlated and capable of spreading.
elements.sent-2411-309: When financial institutions face adverse conditions, such risks can quickly propagate through complex market interconnections, creating a domino effect that escalates losses from a single institution to the entire market, potentially triggering widespread systemic risk.
elements.sent-2411-310: Systemic risk is a core concept in these fields, as it leads to market failures, large-scale financial crises, and profound, long-lasting impacts on the real economy.
elements.sent-2411-311: Typical examples of systemic risk include financial crises, market crashes, bank runs, and contagion effects spreading across multiple industries \citep{divcpinigaitiene2018,zhang2023systemic .}
elements.sent-2411-312: In this paper, we focus on the ``capital'' type of systemic risk model, which is a method to assess systemic risk losses and their probability of occurrence, and can be characterized by a risk measure\footnote{Systemic risk models can be categorized into the following five types: (i) early warning systems (EWS); (ii) capital; (iii) liquidity; (iv) contagion; and (v) network.}
elements.sent-2411-313: For further details, the reader can refer to \citet{silva2017analysis and \citet{ellis2022systemic .. .}}
elements.sent-2411-314: Among widely recognized risk measures, Value-at-Risk (VaR) and Expected Shortfall (ES) have been extensively discussed in the literature \citep{duffie1997,delbaen2000coherent,acerbi2002 .}
elements.sent-2411-315: However, they fail to effectively quantify systemic risk as they only consider isolated individual economic entities.
elements.sent-2411-316: The theoretical properties of these co-risk measures and their applications in finance and insurance can be found in \cite{kritzman2010,asimit2016,feinstein2017,asimit2018systemic,lin2018,duarte2021,liu2021asymptotics,waltz2022vulnerability,yang2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-317: However, these co-risk measures can only assess the interaction effect from one entity to another entity, but cannot characterize the absolute or relative spillover effects, which lays the foundation for relevant research on proposing various risk contribution measures.
elements.sent-2411-318: The class of risk contribution measures can be divided into two types: difference-based and ratio-based contribution measures.
elements.sent-2411-319: The former is usually defined as the difference between conditional and unconditional risk measures, while the latter is defined as the ratio between the difference-based contribution measure and the benchmark unconditional risk measure.
elements.sent-2411-320: From the perspective of stochastic orders and dependence structures, \citet{sordo2018 provided sufficient conditions to rank CoVaR, CoES, and their risk contribution measures $\Delta {\rm CoVaR}$ and $\Delta {\rm CoES}$.}
elements.sent-2411-321: Recently, \cite{zhang2024stochastic introduced several types of ratio-based distortion risk contribution measures ($\Delta^{\rm R {\rm CoD}$) and examined sufficient conditions for comparing these measures in terms of a new characterization of the convex transform order.}}
elements.sent-2411-322: Most of the above-mentioned works only consider a single risk as the systemic risk event, which runs counter to the reality in the financial market that there might be multiple risks collapsing simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-323: In fact, multivariate risks are attracting increasing attention from many researchers such as \cite{lee2013 , \cite{sun2018 , and \cite{ling2019 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-324: The multivariate CoVaR (in short, MCoVaR) and multivariate CoES (in short, MCoES) are firstly formally defined in \cite{ortega2021 to quantify the risk of $X_2,\cdots,X_n$ spilled over to $X_1$.}
elements.sent-2411-325: In this paper, we introduce two types of multivariate risk contribution ratios to reassess the concepts of MCoVaR, MCoES, and MMME.
elements.sent-2411-326: Our research motivation stems from the fact that when we are more concerned with relative contributions rather than absolute contributions of systemic risks, the effectiveness of those contribution measures studied in \cite{ortega2021 is limited.}
elements.sent-2411-327: Therefore, this paper introduces the multivariate risk contribution ratio measures to address this issue, where the first type of benchmark measure uses unconditional risk values such as VaR or ES, which are univariate risk measures, and the second type of benchmark measure uses a multivariate joint risk measure based on the median of systemic events, a method commonly used in financial markets.
elements.sent-2411-328: Based on the newly proposed contribution measures, we theoretically analyze sufficient conditions for comparing two distinct multivariate risk portfolios.
elements.sent-2411-329: In particular, for two risk vectors $(X_1, \dots, X_n)$ and $(Y_1, \dots, Y_n)$, the consistency of co-risk measures is examined under different stochastic orders and dependence assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-330: Further, we compute the values of these new risk measures using real-world dataset in the cryptocurrency market, comparing with existing related risk measures, and analyzing the market interactions.

elements.section-2411-011: An application in the cryptocurrency market
elements.sent-2411-367: In this section, risk measures proposed in Section \ref{sec:mulcontri are applied for cryptocurrency market dataset to quantify the relative spillover effects.}
elements.sent-2411-368: The static approach offers several distinct advantages that align with the goals of this study.
elements.sent-2411-369: Firstly, it delivers clear and interpretable results, allowing for a straightforward comparison of risk contributions among different assets.
elements.sent-2411-370: This clarity is essential for both theoretical validation and practical application, as it provides actionable insights for investors and regulators.
elements.sent-2411-371: Secondly, the static method is computationally efficient, enabling the analysis of large datasets and the application of complex risk measures without excessive resource demands.
elements.sent-2411-372: This efficiency is particularly valuable in the context of cryptocurrencies, where data volumes are substantial and market dynamics are intricate.
elements.sent-2411-373: Lastly, the static approach provides a stable framework for initial exploration and validation of new risk measurement tools, ensuring that the core properties of these tools can be assessed reliably before extending to more complex dynamic analyses .

elements.sent-2411-404: The implications are profound: there exists a pronounced risk interaction in the currency market, characterized by robust correlations among various currencies.
